gaps.before: Hardly had the train left the station
gaps.after: the passengers began to search for their seats.
gaps.explanation: Hardly had ... when ... で「〜するやいなや…」。Hardly と相関するのは when。

gaps.before: After weeks of research, the committee finally
gaps.after: a conclusion about the cause of the problem.
gaps.explanation: draw a conclusion で「結論を導き出す」。conclusion と結びつく動詞は draw。

gaps.before: The road was completely blocked by snow;
gaps.after: , we had to turn back and look for another route.
gaps.explanation: 道がふさがれた→だから引き返した、という因果関係。結果を表す consequently が適切。

gaps.after: more time yesterday, I would have visited the old castle.
gaps.explanation: 過去の事実に反する仮定（仮定法過去完了）。if 節は had + 過去分詞で had had となる。

gaps.after: , carrying our bags and answering every question with a smile.
gaps.explanation: 荷物を運び、笑顔で質問に答えた→「親切な」。文意に合うのは helpful。

readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: The Quiet Work of Bees
paragraphs: When most people picture a bee, they think of honey. Yet the sweet substance in the jar is only a small part of what these insects do. As bees move from flower to flower in search of nectar, they carry grains of pollen on their hairy bodies, allowing plants to produce fruit and seeds.

This service, known as pollination, is worth far more than the honey itself. A large share of the crops we eat — apples, almonds, cucumbers, and many others — depend on insects to reproduce. Without their daily journeys between blossoms, supermarket shelves would look surprisingly bare.

In recent years, however, bee numbers have fallen in many regions. Scientists point to a mix of causes: vanishing wildflowers, certain pesticides, and diseases that spread quickly through crowded hives. Some cities have responded by planting flower-rich gardens and banning the most harmful chemicals, hoping that small local efforts will add up to a real difference.

questions.prompt: 本文の主旨として最も適切なものはどれですか。
options: Bees matter most because of pollination, which many of our crops depend on, and their decline is now a serious concern.
Bees are useful only because they make large amounts of honey for people to eat.
Bees have no real effect on the fruit and vegetables that humans grow.
Scientists agree that bee numbers are rising quickly all over the world.

questions.before: As bees look for nectar, they move pollen between flowers and help plants

questions.prompt: ミツバチの数が減っている原因を示す語句はどれですか。

spans.text: they carry grains of pollen on their hairy bodies
worth far more than the honey itself
vanishing wildflowers, certain pesticides, and diseases

questions.sentence: In other words, these small creatures help put a great deal of our food on the table.

questions.prompt: 第3段落で述べられている内容として最も適切なものはどれですか。
options: Bee numbers have dropped, and some cities are now planting flowers and limiting harmful chemicals to help.
Bee numbers have grown so much that cities are trying to remove them.
Scientists have found no possible reason for the change in bee numbers.
Cities have decided that protecting bees is impossible and have given up.

readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: A Room Full of Free Books
paragraphs: The first public libraries were a quiet revolution. For centuries, books had been costly objects, owned mainly by the rich or kept behind the locked doors of universities. The idea that any ordinary person could walk in, borrow a book, and read it for free seemed almost shocking.

Supporters argued that free access to books would help everyone, not just scholars. A factory worker could study at night, a child could discover faraway places, and a curious mind could follow an interest without paying a penny. Knowledge, they believed, should belong to the whole community.

Today libraries offer far more than printed pages. Many lend films, host language classes, and provide computers for people who have none at home. Yet their original purpose has not changed: to make learning open to all, regardless of how much money a person has.

questions.prompt: 本文が最も伝えたいことはどれですか。
options: Public libraries were created to make books and learning free for everyone, and they still serve that goal today.
Public libraries were built only for wealthy people and university scholars.
Public libraries have stopped lending books and now sell them instead.
Public libraries charge a high fee for every book a person borrows.

questions.before: Before public libraries existed, books were expensive and were owned mainly by

questions.prompt: 現代の図書館が本以外に提供しているものを示す語句はどれですか。

spans.text: a factory worker could study at night
lend films, host language classes, and provide computers

questions.prompt: 本文全体の要点はどれですか。
options: From the start until now, libraries have tried to make learning available to everyone for free.
Libraries have always been private places for the wealthy only.
Libraries are no longer useful because everyone already owns books.
Libraries were created mainly to make money from their readers.

listeningQuestions.transcript: Would you mind sending me the report before you leave the office today?
The train to the airport leaves at a quarter past seven from platform four.
Had I known about the traffic, I would have taken the earlier train instead.
We spent the entire weekend painting the fence and tidying up the garden.
The thin sheet of ice cracked loudly beneath the children's heavy boots.
I am not sure whether the shop is open on Sundays or only on weekdays.
